We will assemble and preprocess a large-scale dataset combining publicly available and in-house generated fluorescence microscopy images annotated with cellular phenotypes under diverse compound treatments, applying rigorous quality control and normalization pipelines. Model performance will be evaluated using quantitative metrics including phenotypic reconstruction accuracy, chemical validity, novelty, and diversity of generated molecules, as well as in vitro experimental validation measuring target engagement, phenotypic modulation, and toxicity in matched cell lines with positive and negative controls, iteratively refining the model through experimental feedback.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates standardized, large-scale phenotypic imaging with molecular generative modeling via disentangled latent representations that explicitly address biological confounders and chemical constraints, enabling interpretable, conditionally optimized drug design grounded in mechanistic cellular responses rather than proxy biochemical metrics alone—advancing beyond prior multi-modal models that lack experimental validation rigor and theoretical clarity on latent alignment and causal inference.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop context-aware molecular generative models that integrate curated, multi-modal, time-resolved biological microenvironment data—capturing dynamic cellular states such as pH fluctuations, metabolite levels, and protein conformational ensembles—to generate drug candidates with improved physiological relevance and functional robustness.

BACKGROUND: Current molecular generative AI models predominantly rely on static or simplified representations of target proteins and biochemical properties, failing to capture the complex, fluctuating microenvironmental conditions that critically influence drug efficacy and stability in vivo. This gap leads to high attrition rates since molecules optimized under static assumptions often do not maintain desired activity across diverse physiological states. The open problem is how to reliably incorporate noisy, heterogeneous, and temporally resolved biological data into molecular generation to produce candidates inherently adapted to dynamic cellular contexts.

APPROACH: We propose to systematically curate and preprocess multi-modal datasets combining single-cell omics (e.g., time-series transcriptomics, metabolomics) and live-cell imaging data that quantify microenvironmental variables linked to molecular interactions under varying conditions. These datasets will be harmonized through imputation and dimensionality reduction techniques tailored to handle missing and indirect measurements, yielding robust contextual embeddings. A generative graph neural network or diffusion-based model will then be conditioned on both static target structures and encoded, time-resolved microenvironmental states, with carefully designed regularization and cross-validation to prevent overfitting and capture causal relationships between context and drug-target interactions.

EXPERIMENT: Validation will involve synthesizing top-ranked candidate molecules and assessing their binding affinities and functional efficacies across controlled, physiologically relevant assays, including organoid cultures and microfluidic devices that recapitulate dynamic tissue microenvironments. Standardized positive and negative controls will be employed, and assay reproducibility will be quantified alongside statistical methods to disentangle compound effects from biological variability.

NOVELTY: This approach uniquely integrates rigorously processed, time-resolved multi-modal biological context into molecular generative models, advancing beyond prior context-aware methods that primarily use static or low-dimensional contextual features. By explicitly addressing data heterogeneity and implementing strategies to ensure causal and generalizable conditioning, it establishes a new paradigm for generating drug candidates optimized for real-world, dynamic cellular environments.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, rigorously validated context-aware multi-modal generative model that integrates temporally resolved cellular phenotypic data with molecular graph representations to design novel compounds optimized for dynamic system-level biological responses and mechanistic efficacy.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI approaches for drug discovery largely rely on static molecular properties or target-centric binding predictions, failing to capture the complex, time-dependent cellular phenotypes that govern therapeutic outcomes and safety. This gap limits the design of molecules that can modulate biological systems dynamically, accounting for pathway crosstalk, adaptive resistance, and off-target effects. Addressing this requires integrating rich, high-dimensional phenotypic data with molecular design under controlled, reproducible experimental frameworks.

APPROACH:  
We propose to construct a large-scale, paired dataset linking molecular graphs with longitudinal multi-modal cellular phenotypes—such as live-cell imaging time series and single-cell transcriptomics—derived from standardized, high-content assays with validated biomarkers and rigorous batch effect controls. A hybrid generative architecture combining graph neural networks for molecular structure generation and transformer-based temporal encoders for phenotypic trajectories will be trained to generate compounds conditioned on desired dynamic phenotypic profiles. The training will incorporate noise-robust objectives and disentanglement constraints to ensure model identifiability and generalization to unseen molecule-phenotype pairs.

EXPERIMENT:  
The model will be evaluated by synthesizing top-ranked generated compounds and testing them in orthogonal cell-based assays, including dose-response and genetic perturbation studies, with inactive analog controls to confirm causal, mechanism-based phenotypic modulation. Performance metrics will include recapitulation of target phenotypic trajectories, pathway activation specificity, and improved efficacy-to-toxicity profiles compared to baseline generative methods.

NOVELTY:  
This approach meaningfully advances prior work by explicitly modeling temporally resolved, system-level phenotypic data within a unified generative framework, supported by a rigorous data generation and validation pipeline that addresses experimental confounders and establishes mechanistic causality. Theoretical and empirical analyses of model invertibility and generalization underpin robust extrapolation to novel molecules and complex phenotypes, a capability largely unexplored in current multi-modal drug design models.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a theoretically grounded federated learning framework that incorporates adaptive, meta-learned context embeddings as dynamic personalization layers, enabling continuous local adaptation to heterogeneous, temporally non-stationary client data without explicit data sharing or excessive communication overhead.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning (FL) faces significant challenges when client data are heterogeneous and non-IID, especially under temporal distribution shifts common in real-world applications like healthcare or edge recommendation systems. Traditional FL aggregation methods and static personalization strategies often fail to capture evolving local data characteristics, resulting in suboptimal personalization accuracy and poor robustness to distributional drifts. There is a critical need for adaptive personalization mechanisms that dynamically respond to both spatial heterogeneity and temporal dynamics while preserving communication efficiency and convergence guarantees.

APPROACH:  
We propose integrating meta-learned context embeddings jointly optimized with model weights during federated training, where each client maintains and updates a low-dimensional embedding capturing latent local data features and temporal shifts. These embeddings parameterize client-specific personalization layers that continuously adapt model outputs without requiring raw data sharing. Formally, we define assumptions on bounded distribution drift and prove convergence guarantees under adaptive embedding updates with controlled communication frequency. We design an embedding update protocol balancing adaptation speed and communication cost, and analyze stability and identifiability to prevent embedding collapse or overfitting.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on publicly available and newly constructed federated benchmarks simulating realistic, non-IID and temporally evolving datasets derived from user interaction logs and sensor streams (e.g., Fedswara, FEMNIST with temporal splits). Metrics include personalization accuracy, convergence rate, communication overhead, and robustness to abrupt distribution shifts quantified by distribution divergence measures. Baselines include FedAvg, FedProx, fixed heuristic context embeddings, and state-of-the-art static personalization methods. Ablations systematically vary embedding dimension and update frequency to isolate their effects.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing FL personalization or meta-learning methods that rely on static or heuristic local adaptation, our approach introduces a theoretically justified, adaptive context embedding mechanism explicitly modeling temporal and distributional heterogeneity with formal convergence and stability guarantees. This framework uniquely balances model personalization quality with practical communication and computational constraints, enabling robust deployment in dynamic, heterogeneous federated environments.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a context-aware personalized aggregation framework for federated learning that dynamically clusters clients based on validated local causal summaries derived via robust causal discovery methods with uncertainty quantification, enabling principled model aggregation that respects heterogeneous causal data-generating processes under realistic federated constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID client data remains fundamentally challenged by differences not only in statistical distributions but also in underlying causal mechanisms across clients, such as varying treatment effects in multi-center healthcare data. Existing personalization and aggregation approaches primarily use statistical similarity or clustering, ignoring the causal structure driving data heterogeneity, which risks conflating spurious correlations and reducing model robustness and fairness. Addressing these challenges requires principled methods that incorporate causal insights while rigorously validating the reliability of causal inferences under realistic data and federation constraints.

APPROACH:  
Each client locally performs causal discovery using scalable, sample-efficient algorithms with built-in uncertainty quantification to infer approximate causal graphs or latent confounder proxies, supported by explicit assumptions on data sufficiency and model class; causal summaries augmented with confidence measures are securely transmitted to the server. The server employs these validated causal summaries to cluster clients into causally homogeneous groups and dynamically adjusts aggregation weights to reflect the inferred causal structures, with fallback or hybrid aggregation methods when causal uncertainty is high. Theoretical analysis characterizes conditions under which causal clustering provably improves global model accuracy and robustness, and quantifies the impact of noisy or misestimated causal inputs on aggregation outcomes.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation uses synthetic federated datasets with explicitly controlled causal heterogeneity and confounding patterns reflecting real-world complexity, alongside curated multi-institutional medical and financial datasets with partial causal annotations. Metrics include model accuracy, robustness to distribution shifts, fairness across client groups, and uncertainty calibration of causal summaries, compared against strong non-causal personalization baselines and ablation studies isolating causal components.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates rigorous local causal discovery with uncertainty quantification into federated aggregation, provides theoretical justification for causal-structure-based clustering in FL, and validates the approach on realistic heterogeneous settings, advancing beyond prior causal federated learning methods that lack uncertainty treatment, strong theoretical grounding, or realistic experimental evaluation.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable adaptive federated client clustering framework that employs continual representation learning of client model updates and metadata to dynamically re-cluster clients in response to evolving non-IID and temporal data distributions, with theoretical guarantees and validated efficiency-performance trade-offs.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID client data faces significant challenges due to stale or inaccurate client grouping, which can degrade both global model generalization and personalization quality, especially when data distributions shift over time. Existing clustering-based FL methods typically rely on static similarity metrics or pre-clustering assumptions that fail to capture temporal dynamics in client behavior and gradient patterns, limiting adaptability and performance in real-world scenarios. Addressing these limitations requires a principled, dynamic clustering mechanism that responds to evolving client data and model updates with provable effectiveness and manageable overhead.

APPROACH:  
We propose a dual-level optimization framework where (1) a continual representation learning module encodes client model updates and relevant metadata into stable, low-dimensional embeddings reflecting both distributional and gradient dynamics under realistic assumptions, and (2) a scalable clustering mechanism periodically reassigns clients based on embedding similarity and cluster-level model performance impact, with formal convergence guarantees under mild assumptions. The framework incorporates complexity-reducing approximations such as embedding update caching and hierarchical clustering to ensure computational and communication overhead remain feasible at scale, while explicitly quantifying trade-offs between clustering frequency and model performance.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate the method on benchmark federated datasets exhibiting temporal and distribution shifts—such as LEAF benchmarks with topic drift and real-world time-series datasets—comparing against fixed clustering, random grouping, and recent dynamic clustering baselines. Metrics will include global model accuracy, convergence speed, cluster cohesion stability (quantified via silhouette scores and cluster drift statistics), personalization gains, and system overhead measured in communication rounds and computation time. Ablation studies will isolate effects of continual representation learning, reclustering frequency, and baseline FedAvg.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines continual, theoretically grounded representation learning of client updates with dynamic clustering that adapts to temporal distributional shifts, supported by formal analyses and practical scalability strategies. Unlike prior static or heuristic clustering approaches, it provides a rigorously validated and operationally efficient framework explicitly designed to manage evolving client heterogeneity in real-world federated systems.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a theoretically grounded federated learning framework that integrates meta-learned contrastive embedding alignment to explicitly unify heterogeneous and non-IID client feature spaces while rigorously handling label space heterogeneity through adaptive embedding projection and providing convergence guarantees.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning (FL) on heterogeneous, non-IID data suffers from representation misalignment across clients due to diverse feature distributions and partially overlapping or disjoint label spaces, which degrades global model generalization and convergence. Current personalization and adaptation methods often rely on local fine-tuning without explicitly aligning client feature representations or providing theoretical assurances on embedding consistency, limiting their effectiveness in highly heterogeneous settings. A crucial open problem is how to learn a shared embedding space that simultaneously preserves local task-specific semantics and reduces cross-client distributional divergence, particularly when client label spaces vary.

APPROACH:  
We propose a federated optimization framework that jointly meta-learns a contrastive embedding module across clients, explicitly aligning heterogeneous feature representations via a global prototype space while incorporating adaptive projection layers to accommodate partial label overlap or disjointness. The method employs a bi-level optimization scheme where at the inner level, clients optimize local contrastive losses to minimize Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and utilize Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) metrics to encourage embedding consistency, and at the outer meta-level, global parameters are updated to improve cross-client embedding alignment and downstream task accuracy. We provide formal analysis characterizing the conditions for embedding space stability and prove convergence guarantees under realistic assumptions about client distribution heterogeneity. Ablation studies isolate the contributions of meta-learning, contrastive loss weighting, and embedding dimensionality.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on federated benchmarks with heterogeneous feature and label distributions, including hospital-specific medical imaging datasets with variable pathology prevalence and user-specific smartphone sensor data with partially overlapping activity labels, comparing against FedAvg, FedProx, Per-FedAvg, and FedRep baselines with matched hyperparameter tuning. Metrics include accuracy, convergence speed, robustness to data skew, as well as quantitative embedding alignment measures (MMD, CKA), supported by synthetic controlled experiments designed to systematically vary label overlap and feature divergence.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines meta-learned contrastive embedding alignment with adaptive projection to explicitly handle heterogeneous label spaces in FL, supported by rigorous theoretical convergence analysis and formal embedding stability conditions, setting it apart from prior federated contrastive and personalization methods which lack such theoretical grounding and do not address label heterogeneity explicitly.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive meta-federated learning framework that dynamically customizes local model architectures and aggregation weights based on formally modeled client data heterogeneity and resource constraints, with rigorously controlled evaluation protocols to improve both personalization and global generalization in heterogeneous, non-IID federated settings.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning (FL) with heterogeneous and non-IID client data faces significant challenges because uniform model architectures and fixed aggregation schemes like FedAvg often lead to degraded convergence and negative transfer. Moreover, client resource variability further complicates training, yet existing FL methods rarely integrate architecture adaptation with resource-aware aggregation in a theoretically grounded manner. Addressing these is critical for practical deployment across diverse edge devices and real-world heterogeneous datasets.

APPROACH:  
We propose a meta-learning-based optimization framework where a meta-controller learns to adapt local neural network architectures (e.g., layer widths and depths) and aggregation weights by profiling explicit, quantifiable client data distribution statistics (label, feature, quantity skews) and computational resource profiles modeled as constraints within the learning objective. This framework jointly optimizes architecture parameters and aggregation strategies, with formal convergence analysis under assumptions of bounded heterogeneity and resource constraints. Architecture search is conducted efficiently with lightweight differentiable modules to minimize overhead, and aggregation adapts dynamically per communication round to balance personalization and global performance.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on standardized federated benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, FEMNIST) and one publicly available medical IoT dataset, using rigorously controlled non-IID partitions that systematically vary label, feature, and quantity skews. Metrics include per-client personalization accuracy on held-out local test sets, global model accuracy, communication cost measured in bytes and rounds, convergence speed, and robustness to client dropout, with multiple randomized seeds to ensure statistical significance. Ablation studies isolate the individual and combined impact of architecture and aggregation adaptation, while resource usage profiling demonstrates feasibility on simulated heterogeneous edge devices.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior FL methods that fix model architectures or rely on static aggregation, our approach jointly integrates resource-aware meta-architecture search and adaptive aggregation within a formal optimization framework supported by convergence guarantees. This bridges meta-learning, architecture adaptation, and practical system constraints, providing a theoretically justified and empirically validated path for scalable personalized FL under severe client heterogeneity.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a hierarchically adaptive deep learning emulator for climate model acceleration that dynamically adjusts spatial and temporal fidelity based on rigorously quantified uncertainty and physical indicators, leveraging a backbone-modulator neural architecture trained on multi-resolution, uncertainty-annotated climate simulation datasets.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models typically operate at fixed resolutions and fidelity levels, causing inefficient allocation of computational resources by over-resolving stable regions while under-representing critical, highly variable areas such as storm genesis zones or ocean circulation tipping points. This limits scalability and accuracy in emulating extreme or abrupt climate phenomena, highlighting the need for uncertainty-aware, adaptive fidelity mechanisms that balance accuracy, uncertainty calibration, and computational cost in high-dimensional, spatiotemporally correlated data.

APPROACH: We propose a two-part neural architecture comprising a coarse-resolution backbone processing global climate fields and a modulator network that identifies spatiotemporal regions requiring enhanced resolution using combined uncertainty estimates from Bayesian deep ensembles and physically motivated gradient indicators (e.g., temperature and precipitation anomaly gradients). Training will utilize large-scale, multi-resolution climate simulation datasets with uncertainty annotations derived from ensemble model outputs, enabling the model to learn adaptive fidelity policies that optimize computational allocation. Theoretical analysis will formalize assumptions on convergence, stability, and uncertainty calibration, and error-cost trade-offs will be rigorously characterized to justify the backbone-modulator design.

EXPERIMENT: The emulator will be evaluated against fixed-fidelity baseline models on benchmark datasets comprising coupled atmosphere-ocean simulations with annotated extreme events and abrupt transitions. Metrics will include emulation accuracy (e.g., RMSE and pattern correlation), uncertainty calibration scores (e.g., expected calibration error), computational cost savings, and ablation studies assessing fidelity thresholds and uncertainty quantification methods.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely integrates theoretically grounded uncertainty quantification with hierarchical spatial-temporal adaptivity in a climate emulator, explicitly formalizing the error-stability-cost trade-offs of adaptive fidelity mechanisms and demonstrating practical, scalable acceleration of climate simulations with rigorous uncertainty calibration—advancing beyond prior fixed-resolution or heuristic adaptive approaches.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a physics-informed, uncertainty-aware deep learning emulator with a theoretically grounded dynamic resolution adaptation mechanism that leverages calibrated uncertainty estimates to efficiently accelerate multi-scale climate model simulations while rigorously preserving physical fidelity.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for climate modeling struggle to balance accuracy and computational efficiency, particularly when capturing complex, multi-scale phenomena such as cloud dynamics or abrupt ocean regime shifts. Fixed spatial and temporal resolutions limit their responsiveness to localized extreme events and regime transitions, while existing uncertainty quantification methods often lack calibration or robustness in high-dimensional climate contexts. There is a critical need for emulators that dynamically allocate computational resources based on reliable uncertainty estimates, without compromising physical consistency or interpretability.

APPROACH:  
We propose an integrated framework combining physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) with Bayesian deep learning ensembles to generate well-calibrated uncertainty estimates that guide a learned controller for adaptive spatial-temporal resolution adjustment. Physics constraints—formulated as conservation laws and empirical physical relationships—are embedded via differentiable loss functions and rigorously analyzed to ensure stability under non-uniform resolution changes. The resolution controller dynamically refines or coarsens emulator fidelity in regions and periods identified by uncertainty thresholds, with fallback hybrid strategies that revert to fixed-resolution inference when uncertainty calibration falls below predefined reliability criteria.

EXPERIMENT:  
The model will be trained on diverse, high-resolution datasets from state-of-the-art climate simulations focusing on tropical cyclones and ocean circulation shifts, employing standardized metrics such as Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) for uncertainty calibration and mean squared error for accuracy. Ablation studies will isolate the impact of physics constraints, uncertainty quantification, and dynamic resolution modules, while controlled experiments will compare against fixed-resolution baselines using reproducible protocols across multiple climate regimes.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely formalizes and validates the coupling of physics-informed learning with uncertainty-aware dynamic resolution adaptation for climate emulation, establishing theoretical guarantees on physical consistency under adaptive scaling and robust uncertainty calibration mechanisms to reliably guide computational resource allocation—overcoming limitations in prior fixed-resolution or heuristic-based approaches.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a physics-informed, multi-scale neural operator framework with integrated Bayesian uncertainty quantification to accurately emulate sub-grid climate processes from high-resolution LES/DNS data, enabling robust and computationally efficient acceleration of Earth System Models (ESMs).

BACKGROUND:  
Capturing sub-grid processes such as cloud microphysics, turbulent convection, and ocean eddies remains a critical challenge in climate modeling due to their complex, multi-scale interactions and inherent stochasticity, which traditional parameterizations approximate with limited accuracy and without uncertainty quantification. Current deep learning emulators often lack explicit enforcement of physical constraints and rigorous generalization assessments, limiting their reliability and interpretability when coupled into ESMs for long-term climate projections.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel neural operator architecture that learns mappings from coarse-grained climate fields to fine-scale sub-grid tendencies by explicitly encoding conservation laws and symmetries via physics-informed regularization terms, while simultaneously modeling multi-scale interactions through hierarchical latent representations. Uncertainty quantification is incorporated via Bayesian deep learning techniques (e.g., variational inference or deep ensembles) to provide predictive confidence, critical for downstream decision-making. The framework formalizes assumptions on scale separation and stochasticity to ensure theoretical consistency and interpretability.

EXPERIMENT:  
The model will be trained on publicly available, high-resolution LES and DNS datasets spanning diverse climate regimes, with training and validation splits designed to probe out-of-distribution generalization. Performance will be rigorously evaluated using quantitative metrics including sub-grid tendency accuracy, conservation error, uncertainty calibration, and long-term stability when coupled into a standard ESM, alongside computational efficiency benchmarks and stress tests under extrapolative climate scenarios.

NOVELTY:  
This work meaningfully advances prior neural operator and physics-informed neural network (PINN) approaches by jointly integrating multi-scale hierarchical representations, strict physical constraint enforcement, and principled Bayesian uncertainty quantification within a unified operator learning framework; it also uniquely provides comprehensive generalization analyses and ablation studies to isolate the impact of each design component on emulator robustness and climate model fidelity.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchically adaptive deep learning emulator for climate models that dynamically modulates spatial and temporal resolutions based on robust, physics-informed regime detection, enabling computationally efficient yet high-fidelity climate simulations across multiple scales.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for climate acceleration operate on fixed spatial grids and uniform time steps, limiting their ability to accurately capture fine-scale processes like convection and cloud microphysics without incurring prohibitive computational costs. Moreover, existing approaches often neglect the complex, nonlocal interactions and abrupt regime transitions inherent in the climate system, reducing their reliability and generalizability. Addressing these challenges requires an adaptive framework that can detect and respond to evolving climate regimes by allocating computational resources dynamically.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical neural emulator architecture combining graph neural networks (GNNs) for spatial adaptivity with temporal convolution and recurrent modules for dynamic temporal scaling. Regime detection will be grounded in physics-based criteria (e.g., stability indices, convective available potential energy) integrated within a meta-learning framework that continuously learns to identify regime transitions from multi-resolution climate simulation data. The emulator will be coupled to a standard global climate model (GCM) via a stable interface designed to manage abrupt resolution changes and mitigate error propagation, employing uncertainty quantification and regularization techniques to enhance stability, prevent overfitting, and maintain physical consistency over long simulations.

EXPERIMENT:  
Training and validation will utilize publicly available multi-resolution datasets such as the DYAMOND project for high-resolution convection-permitting simulations nested within coarser global runs, supplemented by generated multi-scale synthetic data if needed. Performance will be assessed via metrics including root mean square error (RMSE), energy spectrum fidelity, computational cost reduction, and stability over multi-decadal runs. Benchmark tests will cover diurnal cycles, extreme precipitation events, and generalization to unseen climate regimes compared against fixed-resolution emulators and traditional GCM outputs.

NOVELTY:  
This approach uniquely integrates physics-informed regime detection with hierarchical, meta-learned spatial-temporal resolution adaptation within a deep learning emulator, explicitly addressing theoretical assumptions and numerical stability to enable scalable, physically consistent acceleration of climate models beyond fixed-resolution methods.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a physics-informed neural operator framework integrating hard physical constraints and multi-resolution attention to accurately and robustly emulate multi-scale climate subsystems over centennial timescales, validated through rigorous long-term benchmarks and formal stability analyses.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration often neglect explicit enforcement of fundamental conservation laws and face challenges in capturing multi-scale interactions, leading to drift and unphysical solutions in long simulations. This limitation constrains their reliability for extended climate projections, particularly in complex subsystems like tropical convection coupled to large-scale circulation. There is a critical need for emulator architectures with provable stability and conservation properties that can generalize across scales without sacrificing computational efficiency.

APPROACH:  
We propose designing a hybrid neural operator model—combining Fourier Neural Operators and Graph Neural Operators—with embedded differentiable physics modules enforcing conservation of energy, mass, and momentum, alongside multi-resolution attention mechanisms to represent scale interactions explicitly. The architecture will be supported by theoretical analyses demonstrating stability and conservation guarantees, and trained end-to-end on a curated dataset comprising high-fidelity climate simulation outputs paired with synthetically generated, physically consistent data from simplified analytical models designed to enhance training diversity and regularize multi-scale learning. Data preprocessing pipelines and domain adaptation techniques will mitigate domain shift and ensure numerical stability during long-term iterative forecasting.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate the model on a multi-decadal to centennial simulation benchmark targeting tropical convection and its interaction with large-scale circulation, comparing against state-of-the-art black-box emulators and conventional parameterizations. Performance will be assessed via forecast skill scores, conservation error metrics over time, computational speedups, and statistical significance tests. Ablation studies will isolate contributions from physics-informed components and synthetic data, and intermediate validation checkpoints will monitor training stability and generalization.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates provable physical constraints within neural operators tailored for multi-scale climate processes, supported by rigorous experimental protocols and formal theoretical guarantees, thereby closing a key gap in emulator fidelity and robustness for long-term climate acceleration—beyond purely data-driven or loosely constrained approaches.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a theoretically grounded meta-uncertainty network for multi-task deep neural networks that dynamically calibrates task-specific epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties by adapting to evolving task embeddings and distributional shifts, validated through rigorous baselines, ablation studies, and scalable training protocols.

BACKGROUND: Current uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for deep neural networks predominantly provide static uncertainty estimates tailored to single-task settings, failing to capture the complex, time-varying interdependencies and distributional shifts inherent in multi-task learning (MTL) scenarios. This limitation hampers reliable uncertainty calibration and decision robustness in real-world applications where task relevance and data distributions evolve, such as autonomous systems or clinical diagnosis. Addressing how to dynamically and adaptively quantify task-specific uncertainties in MTL under non-stationarity remains an open and critical challenge.

APPROACH: We propose a meta-uncertainty network trained jointly with a multi-task DNN that receives as inputs rigorously defined task embeddings, intermediate feature statistics, and explicit distribution shift representations (e.g., domain discrepancy metrics), and outputs adaptive calibration parameters to modulate base uncertainty estimates. Our framework integrates Bayesian neural networks for epistemic uncertainty and heteroscedastic noise models for aleatoric uncertainty, combined through a decision-theoretic formulation ensuring principled adaptation of uncertainty scaling factors with proven convergence properties under mild assumptions on task embedding stability. To address scalability and data scarcity, we incorporate synthetic distributional shifts and transfer learning, enabling efficient meta-network training and robustness beyond seen distributions.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on benchmark multi-task datasets exhibiting natural and synthetic distributional shifts—such as multi-weather object detection and evolving patient cohort diagnostics—using comprehensive calibration metrics (e.g., Expected Calibration Error, Negative Log-Likelihood), task-wise uncertainty sharpness, and decision robustness benchmarks. Extensive ablation studies isolate contributions from meta-network components and inputs, while comparisons against state-of-the-art static UQ methods (MC dropout, deep ensembles) assess dynamic adaptability.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior static or single-task UQ approaches, this work rigorously formalizes and implements a dynamic, task-aware uncertainty adaptation mechanism grounded in Bayesian and decision-theoretic principles, supported by theoretical guarantees and a scalable training paradigm. It uniquely combines meta-learning with multi-task uncertainty quantification to address real-world non-stationarity, providing a systematic pathway for improved calibration and interpretability in evolving multi-task environments.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a continual uncertainty quantification framework that models uncertainty as a time-dependent stochastic process through a dual-module architecture combining a base DNN and a meta-learned temporal uncertainty estimator, explicitly designed for real-time adaptation to continuous, unpredictable distribution shifts in non-stationary environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) in deep neural networks under dynamic, continuous distribution shifts remains an open challenge, particularly for safety-critical applications like autonomous driving and medical monitoring where data distributions evolve unpredictably over time. Existing methods predominantly assume static or episodic shifts and rely on retrospective calibration, limiting their ability to provide timely, accurate out-of-distribution (OOD) uncertainty estimates during deployment. This gap hinders trustworthy decision-making under real-world temporal non-stationarity.

APPROACH:  
We propose to represent predictive uncertainty as a temporal stochastic process by training a dual-module system: a base neural network producing primary task outputs, and a temporal uncertainty estimator—built with sequential deep models such as transformers or recurrent neural networks—that consumes sequences of prior predictions and uncertainty scores to forecast future uncertainty distributions. Meta-learning techniques will be incorporated to enable rapid adaptation of uncertainty priors using a small sliding window of recent data, explicitly accounting for temporal dynamics and improving robustness to novel domain shifts. Theoretical analysis will formalize assumptions on the distribution shift dynamics and provide bounds or conjectures on calibration and adaptation limits under continual learning.

EXPERIMENT:  
The method will be evaluated on publicly available benchmarks featuring realistic, continuous distribution shifts, including temporally corrupted CIFAR-10 variants and real-world sensor data streams with annotated temporal drifts. We will employ standardized, time-resolved metrics such as temporal expected calibration error, OOD detection AUROC over sequential time windows, and downstream decision robustness under distribution shift. Comprehensive ablation studies will isolate the contributions of temporal modeling, meta-learning, and their integration, with statistical significance testing to ensure reproducibility.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior UQ methods that treat uncertainty as static or episodic and often ignore temporal dependencies, our approach formalizes uncertainty estimation as a dynamic, time-varying process with explicit meta-learned priors for continual adaptation. The theoretical grounding linking temporal uncertainty estimation to Bayesian filtering frameworks and meta-learning principles, alongside rigorous experimental protocols addressing realistic continuous shifts, establishes a new paradigm for trustworthy, real-time uncertainty quantification in non-stationary environments.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a theoretically grounded, context-aware uncertainty recalibration framework for deep neural networks that employs a self-supervised meta-learner to dynamically adapt uncertainty estimates online under realistic, multimodal distribution shifts without requiring labeled data or costly retraining.

BACKGROUND:  
Current uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for deep neural networks typically assume static or known data distributions, leading to poorly calibrated uncertainty estimates when input distributions evolve in deployment scenarios such as sensor drift or environment changes. Existing recalibration techniques often rely on labeled data or static correction methods that fail to adapt to gradual, multimodal, or unforeseen distribution shifts encountered in real-world, non-stationary environments. There is thus an open challenge to maintain reliable and calibrated uncertainty estimates continuously and autonomously in an online or continual learning setup with provable stability guarantees.

APPROACH:  
We propose a dual-stream architecture where the primary predictive model is augmented by a lightweight meta-learner that monitors internal model states (e.g., feature activations, gradient norms) and incoming unlabeled data characteristics, using carefully validated self-supervised objectives such as contrastive and reconstruction losses. The meta-learner dynamically recalibrates uncertainty scaling parameters by detecting subtle distributional shifts, guided by a formal theoretical framework that links the self-supervised signals to calibration degradation proxies and provides stability and convergence guarantees for online adaptation. Ablation studies will isolate the meta-learner’s impact by comparing against standard static methods (e.g., temperature scaling) and alternative recalibration mechanisms, explicitly validating the assumptions under which the self-supervised objectives reliably indicate miscalibration.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on publicly available benchmark datasets exhibiting diverse, well-characterized, and realistic distribution shifts—such as corrupted CIFAR-10 and shifted ImageNet variants with controlled synthetic drifts, as well as temporal real-world datasets including medical time-series and autonomous driving logs with documented sensor/environment changes. Evaluation protocols will measure calibration error (e.g., Expected Calibration Error), prediction accuracy, and robustness metrics over time windows reflecting drift progression, ensuring reproducibility and interpretability by providing precise drift characterizations and publicly releasing code and scripts for continuous evaluation.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates theoretically justified self-supervised signals within an online meta-learning recalibration framework that dynamically maintains uncertainty calibration under realistic, multimodal, and gradual drift without labels or retraining, addressing key limitations of prior static or supervised recalibration methods and providing formal stability analysis rarely available in existing UQ approaches.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a theoretically grounded framework for hierarchical uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks that models and propagates uncertainty through multiple intermediate feature representations using structured variational latent variables, thereby improving calibration, robustness, and interpretability beyond output-level uncertainty methods.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) techniques for deep neural networks, such as Bayesian neural networks and deep ensembles, predominantly focus on uncertainty estimation at the output prediction layer while treating intermediate feature representations as deterministic. This simplification overlooks how input noise, parameter ambiguities, and model approximation errors propagate and potentially amplify uncertainty within the network’s feature hierarchies, leading to overconfident or miscalibrated predictions—an issue critical in safety-sensitive domains like medical imaging and autonomous systems. A principled framework that captures uncertainty at multiple hierarchical levels could provide deeper insight into model behavior and improve reliability under distributional shifts or noisy inputs.

APPROACH:  
We propose embedding structured stochastic latent variables at multiple intermediate layers of a deep neural network, using a hierarchical variational inference scheme with tailored approximate posteriors that explicitly model uncertainty propagation through nonlinear transformations. Our method incorporates carefully designed regularization terms and annealing schedules to mitigate posterior collapse and variance underestimation, ensuring stable training. We will formalize theoretical assumptions about uncertainty composition in feature hierarchies and derive bounds linking intermediate uncertainty fidelity to final prediction calibration and robustness, validated through both analytical proofs and empirical approximations.

EXPERIMENT:  
The framework will be evaluated on hierarchical, multi-scale tasks such as CIFAR-100 hierarchical image classification and the GLUE natural language understanding benchmark, augmented with controlled synthetic noise and structured distribution shifts. We will introduce new quantitative metrics for layer-wise uncertainty calibration (e.g., intermediate Expected Calibration Error variants) and perform ablation studies comparing output-only, selective-layer, and full hierarchical uncertainty modeling. Robustness under label noise and out-of-distribution detection performance will be assessed against strong baselines including deep ensembles and structured Bayesian neural networks.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior Bayesian deep latent variable models that emphasize global latent representations or output-level uncertainty, our approach rigorously integrates hierarchical uncertainty modeling throughout the network’s representational geometry, supported by formal theoretical analysis of uncertainty propagation and practical training stabilizations. This dual theoretical and empirical emphasis on intermediate feature uncertainty distinguishes our work and demonstrates tangible improvements in predictive calibration, robustness, and interpretability over existing methods.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop a meta-learned, context-aware uncertainty calibration framework that dynamically adapts uncertainty estimates in deep neural networks through continual, unsupervised online updates, robustly handling non-stationary domain shifts without requiring ground truth labels during deployment.

BACKGROUND: Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for deep neural networks typically assume a stationary data distribution and rely on fixed calibration techniques, which fail to maintain reliable uncertainty estimates under progressive or subtle domain shifts encountered in real-world, time-evolving environments. The challenge is to create adaptive uncertainty estimators that provide well-calibrated confidence measures in non-stationary settings, while minimizing retraining and dependence on labeled feedback during deployment.

APPROACH: We propose a meta-learning approach that jointly trains a base predictive model and a meta-uncertainty calibration function across multiple source domains with diverse distributional properties, learning to predict dynamic correction factors from recent input feature statistics and model-internal signals (e.g., confidence entropy, loss surrogates). At deployment, the calibration module performs lightweight, continual updates using only unlabeled streaming data and self-supervised performance indicators, adapting uncertainty estimates in real time to detected domain shifts without requiring label supervision or full model retraining.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on standardized continual domain shift benchmarks including CIFAR-10-C, ImageNet-C, and longitudinal medical imaging sequences, employing time-resolved metrics such as expected calibration error (ECE), negative log-likelihood (NLL), and area under the accuracy–confidence curve, alongside computational overhead measurements. Baselines will include state-of-the-art adaptive calibration methods, static calibration techniques, and ablations to rigorously assess adaptation quality, robustness to unseen shift types, and efficiency.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior meta-learning or adaptive calibration approaches that assume stationary environments or require labeled adaptation data, our method explicitly formalizes domain shift conditions under continual adaptation and provides theoretical guarantees on calibration stability without ground truth feedback. This endows the system with practical robustness and flexibility for real-time uncertainty quantification in dynamically evolving, label-scarce scenarios.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a novel multi-modal transformer-based foundation model that integratively learns from raw genomic sequences, matched single-cell epigenomic profiles, and longitudinal single-cell transcriptomic time series to robustly predict cellular differentiation trajectories and elucidate dynamic regulatory mechanisms with high temporal resolution.

BACKGROUND:  
Current foundation models in genomics and single-cell biology primarily focus on static DNA sequences or single-modality transcriptomic snapshots, limiting their capacity to capture the dynamic interplay between genome, epigenome, and gene expression during cellular differentiation. Moreover, existing multi-modal and temporal single-cell models often fail to jointly model raw genomic sequences with concurrent multi-omic time-series data at single-cell resolution, leaving a crucial gap in understanding how static and dynamic molecular layers integrate to direct cell fate decisions. Addressing this gap is essential for mechanistically decoding development and disease progression in a temporally resolved, multi-layered manner.

APPROACH:  
We propose a unified transformer architecture that harmonizes heterogeneous data modalities—raw genomic sequences, single-cell ATAC-seq or methylation profiles, and longitudinal single-cell RNA-seq measurements—through modality-specific encoders and a shared latent space with cross-modal attention mechanisms explicitly designed to preserve temporal context and control for modality-specific noise. Training objectives include masked prediction of missing epigenomic signals from sequence and transcriptomic inputs and autoregressive forecasting of future transcriptomes conditioned on past states and epigenomic context, with regularization strategies such as dropout, data augmentation, and contrastive losses to prevent overfitting. Integration challenges like differing data scales and batch effects will be addressed using normalization layers, domain adversarial training, and explicit temporal embedding.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will curate and/or generate large-scale multi-modal single-cell datasets from established differentiation models (e.g., human hematopoiesis or neural development) with matched single-cell ATAC-seq and RNA-seq collected across multiple developmental time points, complemented by lineage tracing or CRISPR-based perturbations for causal validation. Model performance will be evaluated via reconstruction accuracy of known differentiation trajectories, identification of regulatory elements validated by allele-specific assays, and predictive accuracy of lineage-specific gene expression under perturbations, with batch effect mitigation assessed through replicate consistency metrics.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates raw genomic sequences with temporally resolved multi-omic single-cell data within a unified foundation model that explicitly models cross-modal and temporal dependencies, surpassing prior approaches that treat modalities or time points independently. The methodological innovations in harmonizing heterogeneous scales and enforcing temporal coherence, combined with rigorous experimental validation using causal perturbations, position this model as a first step toward interpretable, dynamic regulatory atlases that link genome sequence to cellular phenotypes over developmental time.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a rigorously validated, multimodal, self-supervised foundation model that integrates single-cell transcriptomics, epigenomics, and spatial imaging data with explicit causal inference modules to robustly infer gene regulatory networks (GRNs) underpinned by well-defined assumptions and harmonized multi-cohort data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell multi-omics analyses often treat modalities in isolation or depend on incomplete supervised labels, limiting discovery of universal and causal gene regulatory principles. Moreover, existing causal inference in single-cell biology frequently lacks explicit assumptions and validation rigor, while data heterogeneity and batch effects further confound integrative analyses. Addressing these gaps is critical for advancing interpretable, predictive models of cell states and regulatory dynamics across diverse biological contexts.

APPROACH:  
We propose to build a large-scale transformer-based foundation model trained on a harmonized atlas of matched single-cell RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, methylation, and spatial transcriptomics/imaging datasets collected under controlled perturbations (e.g., drug treatments, genetic knockouts, developmental time courses) using standardized protocols to minimize batch effects. The model will leverage self-supervised objectives—including masked modality prediction, spatial neighborhood reconstruction, and perturbation effect encoding—augmented with explicit intervention-aware causal inference modules grounded in clearly stated assumptions (e.g., causal sufficiency, no unmeasured confounders). We will incorporate counterfactual prediction and disentangled representation learning architectures to identify direct regulatory interactions robust to confounding, supported by theoretical identifiability analyses and simulation studies.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validation will utilize multiple well-characterized perturbation datasets with gold-standard GRNs (e.g., CRISPR-based knockouts, time-resolved developmental atlases) to benchmark causal effect inference accuracy using metrics such as precision-recall for edge recovery, counterfactual prediction error, and robustness across batches. Ablation studies will assess the impact of causal modules and data harmonization strategies on model performance and biological interpretability.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines a rigorously validated causal inference framework with a multimodal foundation model trained on carefully standardized, perturbation-rich single-cell multi-omics and spatial data, surpassing prior transformer or graph-based integration methods by embedding theoretically grounded causal discovery within a self-supervised architecture optimized for joint modality and spatial context learning. This enables more accurate, interpretable, and generalizable GRN inference with direct application to precision medicine and developmental biology.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable transformer-based foundation model that jointly integrates harmonized, multi-modal single-cell datasets—including transcriptomics, epigenomics, spatial, and proteomic data—across diverse tissues and conditions, with novel hierarchical latent variable architectures and biologically grounded pretraining objectives to learn universal, context-aware genomic representations enabling robust transfer and mechanistic interpretability.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell analyses largely treat modalities and biological contexts independently, limiting the discovery of intricate regulatory and cellular state relationships that emerge from integrated multi-modal views. Existing approaches often lack principled mechanisms to disentangle shared versus context-specific signals while addressing batch effects and technical variability across large heterogeneous datasets. A scalable, interpretable model that can unify these data modalities and generalize across tissues, developmental stages, and species remains an open challenge crucial for advancing precision biology.

APPROACH:  
We will assemble and harmonize a large-scale, multi-modal single-cell atlas integrating matched transcriptomics, ATAC-seq, DNA methylation, spatial transcriptomics, and protein expression from multiple tissues, developmental timepoints, and disease states, applying rigorous batch correction and orthogonal validation via perturbation datasets. The foundation model will employ a transformer architecture augmented with hierarchical latent variables explicitly modeling modality-specific noise and shared biological variation, guided by pretraining objectives such as masked modality reconstruction, cross-modal contrastive learning, and spatial neighborhood prediction, each theoretically grounded to promote disentangled, biologically meaningful embeddings. Computational scalability will be ensured via sparse attention and distributed training, while interpretability will be enhanced by integrating attention visualization and post hoc latent space factorization linked to known regulatory features.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate the model’s transfer learning performance on downstream tasks including rare cell-type classification, regulatory element activity prediction, and cell-cell communication inference across held-out tissues and species, quantitatively benchmarking against state-of-the-art modality-specific and multi-modal methods using domain-relevant metrics (e.g., F1 score, AUPR) and validating biological predictions through independent perturbation and imaging datasets.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines hierarchical latent variable transformers with biologically motivated, multi-objective pretraining tailored for large-scale, harmonized multi-modal single-cell data, explicitly modeling modality-specific biases and spatial context to disentangle universal and context-dependent genomic signals—advancing beyond prior models that either handle modalities separately or lack rigor in noise modeling and biological validation.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a rigorously validated multimodal transformer-based foundation model that integrates matched single-cell transcriptomic, epigenomic, and spatial data from temporally-resolved perturbation experiments to accurately predict dynamic cell state transitions and lineage trajectories.

BACKGROUND:  
Current foundation models in single-cell genomics typically focus on single-modality data (e.g., RNA-seq alone), limiting their ability to capture the complex regulatory interplay driving cell fate decisions. Moreover, existing multimodal integration methods often lack explicit modeling of temporal dynamics and lineage structure, and are rarely trained on truly matched multimodal datasets, constraining their predictive power for dynamic biological processes. This gap hampers mechanistic understanding and predictive modeling of cellular responses to perturbations, which are essential for precision medicine applications.

APPROACH:  
We will assemble a large-scale, rigorously controlled multimodal single-cell dataset comprising matched RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, and spatial transcriptomics profiles from the same cells or tightly synchronized cell populations across multiple tissues and timepoints following diverse perturbations (e.g., drug treatment, CRISPR knockouts). Batch effect mitigation will be implemented via integrated experimental design (e.g., multiplexing, spike-ins) and computational normalization. The foundation model will employ a novel hierarchical transformer architecture with modality-specific encoders and cross-modal attention layers that explicitly model noise and missing data via probabilistic embeddings. Temporal dynamics will be incorporated through learned latent trajectories constrained by experimentally derived pseudo-temporal ordering and lineage tracing data. Training objectives include cross-modal reconstruction, temporal state prediction under perturbation, and lineage inference, grounded in probabilistic graphical model assumptions to ensure interpretability and biological plausibility.

EXPERIMENT:  
The model will be evaluated on benchmark datasets with matched modalities and ground-truth lineage information, using metrics including imputation accuracy, temporal trajectory inference quality (e.g., Kendall’s tau), and lineage tree reconstruction fidelity. Experimental validation will involve prospective perturbation assays combined with in vivo lineage tracing in organoid or mouse models to test predicted cell state transitions and fate outcomes.

NOVELTY:  
This work pioneers a theoretically grounded, temporally explicit multimodal foundation model trained on truly matched single-cell multi-omics data with integrated spatial context and lineage information—surpassing prior multimodal frameworks that lack temporal modeling or rely on separately measured modalities. The approach’s combination of advanced transformer architectures with rigorous experimental design and validation establishes a new standard for dynamic, mechanistic inference in single-cell biology.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, interpretable transformer-based foundation model that jointly embeds rigorously matched multi-modal single-cell omics (scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq, CITE-seq) and spatial transcriptomics data to accurately infer continuous cellular phenotypic states and lineage trajectories within complex tissue architectures.

BACKGROUND:  
Current foundation models for single-cell biology largely focus on transcriptomic data alone and often disregard complementary modalities such as epigenomics, proteomics, and spatial context, limiting their resolution of subtle and dynamic cellular phenotypes. Integrating heterogeneous multi-omic and spatial data remains an open challenge due to technical variability, batch effects, and missing modalities, which impedes accurate modeling of cell-state continua and spatiotemporal dynamics critical for understanding processes like differentiation and tumor microenvironment remodeling.

APPROACH:  
We will design a multi-modal transformer architecture with modality-specific encoders and cross-attention mechanisms that jointly embed matched single-cell transcriptomic, epigenomic, proteomic, and spatial data from the same samples, incorporating explicit modeling of batch and technical covariates. The model will be trained on a comprehensive human hematopoiesis and tumor progression atlas, leveraging rigorous data harmonization and imputation strategies to handle missing modalities and minimize noise. Interpretability will be enhanced through attention weight analysis and embedding disentanglement, while scalability will be ensured via efficient sparse attention and distributed training pipelines optimized for large-scale single-cell datasets.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will validate model performance by benchmarking cell-type and state classification accuracy against gold-standard lineage tracing datasets and perturbation experiments, assessing imputation fidelity for missing modalities, and evaluating reconstruction of known lineage trajectories and spatial cell neighborhood patterns. Performance metrics will include prediction accuracy, embedding interpretability scores, scalability benchmarks, and robustness to batch effects.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates multi-modal single-cell and spatial data through a rigorously validated, theoretically grounded transformer framework that explicitly models batch effects and missing data, combining interpretability with scalability at an unprecedented scale. Unlike prior methods that treat modalities independently or rely on simplistic concatenation, our model jointly learns unified embeddings preserving complex molecular-spatial relationships, enabling detailed reconstruction of continuous cell phenotypes and their spatial-temporal evolution within native tissue contexts.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a formal multi-agent reinforcement learning framework for emergent communication that enables agents to contextually ground evolving semantic meanings across dynamically changing multi-modal environments, balancing stability and plasticity to achieve robust, transferable, and compositional communication protocols.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication research predominantly assumes fixed-task or static-environment settings, limiting agents’ ability to adapt communication protocols as environmental context and semantic mappings change over time. A fundamental open problem is enabling agents to incrementally learn and update shared languages that generalize across varying sensory modalities and shifting semantic relevance, akin to human language adaptability, which is critical for deploying multi-agent systems in real-world non-stationary environments. Existing continual learning and emergent communication frameworks lack formal mechanisms to model and evaluate such dynamic contextual grounding and semantic evolution.

APPROACH:  
We propose a formalization of *contextual grounding* as a latent variable model linking multi-modal perceptual inputs to emergent symbols whose semantic mappings evolve according to an underlying non-stationary environment distribution. Agents will be trained in a staged multi-agent reinforcement learning framework where each phase introduces new sensory modalities and systematically alters semantic reward structures and signal relevance. A modular environment design combined with curriculum learning will facilitate incremental learning while specialized regularization methods (e.g., elastic weight consolidation variants) will enforce a principled stability-plasticity trade-off during protocol updates. We will explicitly model semantic drift and establish mechanisms for agents to detect and adapt to these shifts, enabling continual language refinement without catastrophic forgetting.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate agents in a simulated multi-modal environment (e.g., visual, audio, tactile), progressively adding modalities and semantic shifts in discrete phases. Quantitative metrics will include emergent communication success rates, transfer learning performance across phases, compositionality scores adapted from topological similarity and mutual information measures that account for evolving semantics, and standardized interpretability evaluations using probing classifiers. Control experiments will isolate effects of environmental changes versus agent adaptation dynamics by fixing or perturbing modalities and semantics independently.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely formalizes and operationalizes the concept of *contextual grounding with evolving semantics* in emergent communication, explicitly integrating multi-modal continual learning with stability-plasticity mechanisms in multi-agent systems. Unlike prior emergent communication or continual learning studies that treat semantic mappings as static or unimodal, our approach models and empirically validates communication adaptability under non-stationary, multi-modal semantic drift, representing a foundational advance toward deploying adaptable, lifelong-learning multi-agent communicative AI.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and analyze context-adaptive, dual-modality communication protocols in multi-agent systems that dynamically regulate message complexity and channel selection based on formalized environmental uncertainty and task difficulty metrics.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing emergent communication research predominantly focuses on fixed symbolic or single-channel protocols, limiting agents’ ability to flexibly adjust communication strategies in dynamic, partially observable environments. The open problem is how to enable agents to autonomously develop communication systems that are both emergent and context-sensitive, leveraging multiple modalities with explicit trade-offs in cost and reliability, while maintaining interpretability and generalizability grounded in rigorous theoretical frameworks.

APPROACH:  
We formalize context-adaptivity as a policy mapping from quantified environmental uncertainty (entropy-based) and task complexity (measured by defined difficulty indices) to communication channel selection and message encoding strategies across two modalities: discrete symbolic tokens and continuous neural embeddings. Agents optimize a multi-objective reinforcement learning objective balancing task success and communication cost, with ablations isolating the effect of channel switching and message compositionality. Communication expressiveness and compositionality are quantified using information-theoretic metrics (e.g., mutual information, topological similarity) to ensure interpretability.

EXPERIMENT:  
We implement a cooperative navigation task with heterogeneous agents in a partially observable gridworld, systematically varying sensory noise and task complexity, and evaluate emergent protocols using metrics of communication efficiency, adaptability, compositionality, and task performance against fixed-modality and non-adaptive baselines.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates formal uncertainty and task difficulty quantification with dual-modality communication under a multi-objective learning framework, providing the first systematic empirical and theoretical investigation into how agents can dynamically regulate communication modes and message complexity in emergent protocols.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Investigate the emergence of context-dependent communication protocols in multi-agent systems by explicitly modeling and quantifying dynamic social hierarchies and trust relationships using validated metrics that shape language complexity and social pragmatics.

BACKGROUND: Existing research on emergent communication in multi-agent systems largely focuses on task-driven language protocols without systematically integrating evolving social constructs such as leadership roles and trust dynamics. However, human language intricately reflects social structures through politeness, jargon, and dialectical variation, suggesting that incorporating measurable social cognition into artificial agents could yield richer, more adaptive communication. The open problem lies in defining, quantifying, and leveraging dynamic social variables to guide the formation and evolution of emergent languages in a stable, interpretable manner.

APPROACH: We propose to formalize social constructs by designing reproducible algorithms for assigning and updating agent roles (e.g., leader, follower, mediator) via performance-based metrics and negotiation protocols, alongside trust scores derived from interaction history using validated measures such as Bayesian trust models or reputation systems. These social variables will influence both communication prioritization and message interpretation within an initially unconstrained symbolic channel. To ground the approach theoretically, we will incorporate inductive biases—such as role-conditioned message encoding and social-influence weighted loss functions—that encourage the emergence of socially nuanced linguistic features including politeness markers and role-specific jargon. Environmental constraints and training schedules will be crafted to ensure stable learning dynamics, with controlled timing for role rotations and trust updates.

EXPERIMENT: Experiments will be conducted in a multi-agent cooperative environment featuring repeated communication-dependent tasks, with systematic role assignment schedules and trust update mechanisms. We will evaluate emergent language complexity via syntax depth, semantic shift metrics, and pragmatics indicators (e.g., politeness expression frequency), correlating these with social topology changes over time. Ablation studies and baseline setups without social structure dynamics will isolate their causal effects on language evolution.

NOVELTY: This work meaningfully advances prior emergent communication research by rigorously integrating explicitly defined, quantifiable social constructs and validated update algorithms into multi-agent learning frameworks, supported by theoretically grounded inductive biases. It uniquely enables the study of how dynamic social hierarchies and trust networks drive the development of socially contextualized and pragmatically rich communication protocols.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a meta-learning framework enabling decentralized multi-agent systems to autonomously learn, adapt, and switch communication protocols with quantifiable efficiency and reuse across diverse, dynamic multi-task environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing emergent communication research largely centers on fixed or task-specific protocols in static environments, limiting agents’ flexibility to handle multiple, changing objectives without centralized control. A critical open problem is defining and enabling autonomous, context-dependent protocol adaptation and switching that balance communication efficiency and generalization across tasks, grounded in formal measures and scalable to realistic multi-agent settings.

APPROACH:  
We propose a modular meta-learning architecture where agents jointly optimize task-level communication protocols alongside a higher-level policy that governs decentralized switching or blending of these protocols based on observable contextual embeddings. Communication efficiency and protocol reuse will be operationalized using normalized mutual information and entropy-based metrics, while adaptation will be quantified via transfer performance and protocol divergence measures. Theoretical analysis will establish sufficient conditions for stable protocol switching without centralized coordination, leveraging concepts from information bottleneck theory and decentralized decision processes.

EXPERIMENT:  
Agents will be evaluated in a sequence of controlled, simulated cooperative tasks (navigation, resource allocation, negotiation, joint construction) with systematically varying environmental parameters and bandwidth constraints. Performance will be measured by task success rate, bits transmitted per task normalized by task complexity, protocol reuse indices, and adaptation speed, benchmarked against ablations including fixed-protocol agents and single-task baselines.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates rigorous, information-theoretic metrics with a theoretically grounded, decentralized meta-learning approach to emergent communication, explicitly addressing the challenge of context-aware protocol switching and reuse across heterogeneous tasks—an aspect underexplored in prior multi-agent communication literature.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop and rigorously evaluate a theoretical and empirical framework for context-adaptive emergent communication protocols in heterogeneous multi-agent systems operating under dynamically shifting environmental constraints.

BACKGROUND: Existing emergent communication research predominantly focuses on fixed or slowly changing environments with homogeneous agents, resulting in static signaling protocols optimized for narrow task settings. However, real-world multi-agent systems—such as drone swarms or decentralized sensor networks—must operate under rapidly evolving conditions and diverse agent capabilities, necessitating communication strategies that adapt flexibly and autonomously without centralized control. Addressing how communication protocols emerge and continuously adapt in such non-stationary, heterogeneous contexts remains an open challenge.

APPROACH: We propose a formal theoretical model characterizing how agent heterogeneity and environmental dynamics jointly influence the emergence and adaptation of communication protocols, explicitly modeling assumptions about agent sensory/computational constraints and environmental variability. Empirically, we implement a multi-agent simulation with incrementally increasing task complexity, agent heterogeneity (sensory modalities, compute power, action sets), and environment non-stationarity, training agents with combined reinforcement learning and meta-learning architectures designed to enable continual protocol refinement. We incorporate controlled communication bottlenecks and noise to assess robustness, and employ ablation studies contrasting static vs. dynamic environments, homogeneous vs. heterogeneous agents, and meta-learning vs. standard RL.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation metrics include quantitatively defined communication efficiency (information throughput normalized by bandwidth), task performance (success rates and completion times), and adaptation speed (time to regain baseline performance after environmental or team composition changes). Experimental controls include baseline protocols learned in static settings and homogeneous populations.
